concerns.text: Acceptance Scenario 1 uses '[deferred]' for all critical parameters (elements, FPR, unique strings, queries). Without concrete values or a defined procedure to derive them, this scenario is not testable. A test cannot be written to verify 'at least 99% of queries' if the input size and target FPR are unknown.

Acceptance Scenario 2 sets a fixed false positive limit (11%) but relies on '[deferred]' for the number of non-member strings queried. The statistical validity of the 11% claim depends on the sample size (N). Without a specified N or a procedure to determine it, the success criterion is unmeasurable.

Acceptance Scenario 1 references a dataset size of '[deferred]'. The requirement to 'record peak memory usage' cannot be verified if the dataset size is undefined. The spec must specify the range of sizes (e.g., 10k to 1M) or a specific test case size to make this verifiable.

Acceptance Scenario 2 requires recording time for '[deferred]' membership queries. The precision requirement (3 decimal places) is meaningless without a defined query count. The testability of this scenario fails because the workload is undefined.

FR-002 mandates configuring FPR targets at '[deferred], [deferred], [deferred]'. While the Constitution (Principle VI) defines these as 1%, 5%, 10%, the Functional Requirement itself must be self-contained and verifiable. Relying on an external document for the specific values makes the FR untestable in isolation.

FR-004 requires recording time for '[deferred]' membership queries. Similar to FR-002, the specific workload count is missing from the requirement text, making it impossible to verify if the system meets the requirement without cross-referencing external assumptions.

SC-004 defines reproducibility as 'coefficient of variation ≤15% across 5 repetitions'. However, the spec does not define the dataset size or FPR for which this CV applies. A CV of 15% might be acceptable for small datasets but unacceptable for large ones. The metric lacks the necessary context (the 'what' and 'under which conditions') to be measurable.
